title: Entanglement capacity of complex networks from quantum walks
abstract: Discrete-time quantum walks provide a natural framework for quantum transport on complex networks. On regular structures, coin-walker entanglement has been widely used to characterize quantum transport and to support quantum algorithmic protocols. However, this notion relies on a fixed Hilbert space factorization separating coin and position and is therefore not directly applicable to more complex, irregular structures. Here we introduce an entanglement measure for general networks based on a bipartition that assigns each node two roles, acting as both a source and a target. The resulting bipartition defines the source-target entanglement, a measure for general networks, motivated by coin-walker entanglement. We show that the connectivity of the network imposes an upper bound on this entanglement and identify graph matchings as the underlying structure governing entanglement generation. We further illustrate that in random graphs improving graph connectivity reduces the attainable entanglement, establishing a structure-dependent constraint on quantum correlations.
summary: The paper proposes a new entanglement measure, 'source-target entanglement,' that works for quantum walks on irregular networks where standard methods fail. It reveals that the maximum entanglement a network can support is limited by its graph-theoretic matching size and that higher connectivity or clustering can actually hinder entanglement generation.
main_problem: Quantifying entanglement in discrete-time quantum walks on irregular or complex networks where traditional coin-walker entanglement is not well-defined due to varying node degrees.
main_result: The authors introduce 'source-target entanglement' and prove that the entanglement capacity of a graph is upper-bounded by the logarithm of the size of the largest matching in its bipartite double cover, finding that increased connectivity and clustering actually suppress entanglement.
method: The study uses a bipartite double cover construction to define a new entanglement measure and employs graph theory (matching theory) alongside numerical simulations on Erdős–Rényi and Barabási–Albert random graphs.
model_or_system: Discrete-time quantum walks (DTQWs) on complex networks, specifically using symmetric digraphs, Erdős–Rény_en (ER) graphs, and Barabási–Albert (BA) graphs with Grover coin dynamics.
key_observables: Source-target entanglement (S_st), coin-walker entanglement, and graph matching size.
important_parameters: Network connectivity (p for ER, m for BA), clustering coefficient, and time evolution steps.
main_assumptions: The study relies on the bipartite double cover as a theoretical proxy for the coin-walker division and focuses on symmetric digraphs.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the bipartite double cover construction and the impact of clustering; Figure 2 compares different coin assignments; Figure 3 shows the time evolution of entanglement and its anti-correlation with the clustering coefficient in random graphs.
paper_structure: The paper introduces a new entanglement measure for irregular graphs, establishes a theoretical upper bound based on graph matchings, demonstrates the tightness of this bound, and then explores how network topology (connectivity and clustering) affects entanglement dynamics in random graph models.
why_interesting: This work provides a new way to characterize quantum correlations in complex topologies, which is highly relevant for understanding how quantum information spreads in non-regular quantum networks and many-body systems with complex connectivity.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Illustration DTQW states and their source- target entanglement: (a) A generic graph G, composed of eight nodes and twelve edges. Arrows highlight edges that contribute to a DTQW state defined on the symmetric di- graph G, cf. Eq. (1). (b) Bipartite double cover of the graph G from panel (a). Each node n is split into a source and target (n±) and states |n →m⟩are mapped to |n+⟩⊗|m−⟩. The bold red edges form a maximum matching of B(G) and correspond to the DTQW state illustrated in panel (a). (c) Source-target entanglement generated during the time evolu- tion of a DTQW on networks with different structural prop- erties. Low clustering, hub dominated structures yield higher...

FIG. 2. Entanglement of Haar random states on regu- lar graph. We consider the three-regular symmetric digraph in panel (a) and generate Haar random states in the arc ba- sis. In panel (b) we evaluate the source-target entanglement of ten states (colored cross) and compare it to the coin-walker entanglement for all (3!)6 = 46656 distinct coin assignments (scatter lines, black crosses indicating averages).

FIG. 3. Source–target entanglement in random graphs. Panels (a) and (b) show the time evolution of the source-target entanglement entropy Sst for ER and BA graphs respectively. Faint lines depict the entanglement entropy for 100 different graph realizations and the solid line is the average of these realizations. We observe that DTQWs on both network models exhibit higher entanglement at low connectivity, whereas increasing connectivity reduces the entanglement. Panels (c) and (d) present scatter plots of the time-averaged plateau values from panels (a) and (b) against the average clustering coefficient C of each realization, for the ER and BA models, respectively.
